claim: Free-roaming farm dogs have core home ranges of 0.54-0.89 km² (50% KDE) and full ranges of 2.72-3.42 km² (95% KDE), measured by GPS on a 100-hectare Welsh farm.
stance_target: quantitative home range for free-roaming dogs as baseline for search area models
scope_conditions: Three farm dogs (Springer Spaniel, Huntaway-Collie cross, Huntaway in training) on 100ha mixed farm, mid Wales, Sept 2022 and March 2023
quoted_support: Dog 3 had the smallest core and full range size (0.54 km² and 2.72 km² respectively) compared to dog 1 (0.89 km² and 3.38 km² respectively) and dog 2 (0.77 km² and 3.06 km² respectively).

claim: Farm dogs spent 60-71% of their time within 25 meters of field boundaries, demonstrating strong boundary-following movement behavior.
stance_target: terrain corridor modeling — dogs preferentially follow linear boundaries
scope_conditions: Three GPS-tracked free-roaming farm dogs, Wales, 100ha mixed farm
quoted_support: The dogs were frequently detected close to the field boundaries, particularly dogs 1 and 2, with 71, 70 and 60% of total time across the study duration spent within a 25 m radius for dogs 1, 2 and 3, respectively.

claim: Free-roaming dogs spend 64-74% of active time at slow speeds (<50 m/min) and only 11-16% at fast speeds (≥100 m/min), with average movement rates of 48-51 m/min.
stance_target: quantitative speed parameters for dog movement ecology models
quoted_support: Dog 1 spent notably less time on slow movements (64%) compared to dogs 2 and 3 (74 and 72% respectively)... dog 1 spent more time on fast movements (16%) compared to dogs 2 and 3 (11 and 11% respectively)... movements of 50.90 meters/min (m/m) were calculated for dog 1, 48.66 m/m for dog 2, and 51.09 m/m for dog 3.

claim: Domestic dogs roam further in the dry season than in the wet season, with evidence from studies in northern Australia (Torres Strait and Northern Peninsula Area).
stance_target: seasonal/weather effects on dog roaming range — directly relevant to Algarve summer vs winter
scope_conditions: Free-roaming dogs in tropical/subtropical climates with distinct dry/wet seasons (Australia)
quoted_support: Studies have indicated that dogs roam further in the dry season compared to the wet season in the Torres Strait and a Northern Peninsula Area of Australia.
region: Torres Strait and Northern Peninsula Area, Australia

claim: Working dogs exhibit more consistent day-to-day spatial patterns (mean Bhattacharyya coefficient 0.67) compared to pet dogs with freedom to roam (mean BC 0.27), because work is routinely situated in similar locations.
stance_target: predictability of dog movement patterns based on role/routine
quoted_support: We observed the most similarity in space-use between days for dog 2 (mean BC of 0.67 across all days) and the least similarity between days for dog 1 (mean BC of 0.27 across all days). This may be because dog 2 was an experienced working dog, and work may have been situated in similar locations, whereas dog 1 was a pet dog with a greater ability to roam further.

claim: A previous study found domestic dogs preferentially use anthropogenic features including roads, but this varies by individual.
stance_target: road corridor preference as terrain movement pattern in free-roaming dogs
scope_conditions: Free-roaming domestic dogs across multiple settings (citation: Cunha Silva et al. 2022)
quoted_support: A previous study found that domestic dogs have a preference for anthropogenic areas including roads, but this may vary on an individual basis.
region: Rabies endemic countries, rural and urban settings
